flashcards.back: A linear data structure where elements (nodes) are stored non-contiguously in memory and connected via pointers.

1. Data - Stores the value
2. Next - Pointer to next node

(Doubly linked lists also have 'prev' pointer)

O(n)

Must traverse from head to reach any element. No direct access like arrays.

O(1)

Create new node, point to current head, update head. No shifting required.

1. Singly Linked (one-way)
2. Doubly Linked (two-way)
3. Circular Singly
4. Circular Doubly

1. Dynamic size
2. O(1) insert/delete at known position
3. No memory wastage
4. Easy implementation of stacks/queues

1. No random access (O(n))
2. Extra memory for pointers
3. Poor cache performance
4. Reverse traversal difficult (singly)

flashcards.front: What does NULL indicate in linked list?
flashcards.back:
End of the list. The last node's 'next' pointer is NULL.

Also indicates an empty list (head == NULL).
